paper_title: Global Imbalances in International Trade, Dynamics of Debt and Finance: Causes and Mitigation Measures
research_answer: The US tariffs introduced on Chinese goods starting in 2018 did reduce the bilateral trade deficit with China, but it remains very large. The US-China goods trade deficit peaked at $418.2 billion in 2018, the same year tariffs were introduced. By 2024, it had declined to $295.4 billion — a reduction of roughly $123 billion, or about 29%. However, this deficit still represents 24.4% of the entire US trade deficit, making China by far the single largest source of the US trade imbalance. Meanwhile, the overall US current account deficit actually widened to a record $1.13 trillion in 2024, growing 25.2% in a single year — suggesting that reducing the China-specific deficit has not arrested the broader US trade imbalance, possibly because imports shifted to other trading partners rather than being replaced by domestic production.
supporting_quotes: The US trade deficit with China rose substantially after China's Accession to the World Trade Organisation in 2001 and reached its historical maximum $418,2 billion in 2018. After the US introduced higher tariffs on imports from China in 2018, the US-China trade balance deficit began to decline, eventually reaching $295.4 billion in 2024.
This deficit accounts for 24.4% of the total US trade deficit.
It widened by 25.2% in 2024 and reached its highest historical volume of $1.13 trillion or 3.9% of GDP.
